fuzzy_prd:
We need a small library for managing the contents of signed authentication tokens used across our platform. The library should handle four main concerns: checking whether a compact token string is structurally well formed, providing an immutable container for the named data fields carried inside a token, validating that a set of data fields is complete and temporally fresh, and assembling a complete set of data fields from whatever partial information the caller provides.

For the structural check, the library should accept a compact string only when it contains the exact right number of dot-separated sections — anything fewer or greater should be rejected immediately.

For the immutable container, developers should be able to read data by field name, retrieve multiple field values at once, check whether certain fields exist or contain specific values, and fetch a field by its well-known semantic role (such as subject, issuer, or token identity). Any attempt to modify or remove entries from the container must be blocked, consistent with how we handle immutability in the permissions module.

For validation, the library must verify that all required standard fields are present and that the relevant timestamps are within the valid window — expiration cannot have passed, and future-dated fields are not accepted.

For assembly, the library should auto-populate any standard fields the caller leaves out, using a configurable lifetime in minutes that defaults to sixty. The token identity field must be derived deterministically from the subject and the not-before values when not explicitly supplied. Caller-supplied fields must appear first in the output, maintaining their original order, with auto-generated fields appended after.

A couple extra specifics from the team’s questions: on the compact token format, we only accept it when there are exactly 3 dot-separated segments, like 'foo.bar.baz'. If there are fewer or more than 3 segments, we reject it with error=token_invalid. Also, for the payload itself, structural validation only passes when all six required claims are present: iss, iat, exp, nbf, sub, jti. If even one is missing, that also comes back as error=token_invalid.

On payload assembly, the default TTL is 60 minutes. exp should be calculated from the reference current time, now, plus 60 minutes, unless the caller overrides that by sending a ttl field in the make_payload request. And for jti, when the caller does not provide it, we generate it as md5('jti.<sub>.<nbf>'), using the assembled sub and nbf values. The concrete example here is sub=1 and nbf=123, which gives jti = md5('jti.1.123') = 'd1f6bff329cfc64b2503d2fc3e259583'.

One other detail on output formatting: the payload is serialized with PHP's json_encode and forward slashes stay escaped, so 'http://example.com/foo' shows up as 'http:\/\/example.com\/foo' in the JSON output. The emitted payload is a JSON object with no extra whitespace, followed by a newline. And on immutability, the Payload class implements ArrayAccess and blocks writes and unsets by throwing a PayloadException from offsetSet() and offsetUnset(); in the adapter that gets surfaced as 'error=immutable'.

trigger_keywords: what order do claims appear in the assembled payload
are caller claims first or generated claims first
how is claim ordering maintained in make_payload
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Caller-supplied claims appear first in the output, in the exact order they were provided. Auto-generated standard claims (iss, iat, exp, nbf, jti) are appended after, but only for those not already supplied by the caller. For example, if the caller provides {sub, jti, iat}, the output order is: sub, jti, iat (caller-supplied), then iss, exp, nbf (auto-generated missing ones).

trigger_keywords: what are the typed claim accessor names
what type names map to which claims
which type strings are supported in payload_get_typed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The supported type names and their mappings are: 'subject' -> getSubject (sub), 'issuer' -> getIssuer (iss), 'expiration' -> getExpiration (exp), 'not-before' -> getNotBefore (nbf), 'issued-at' -> getIssuedAt (iat), 'audience' -> getAudience (aud), 'jwt-id' -> getJwtId (jti). Requesting an unsupported type returns error=unknown_claim.

trigger_keywords: what happens when you try to modify a payload
what error is returned when mutating a claim set
can you unset a claim from the payload
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any attempt to set or unset a key in the Payload container (via ArrayAccess offsetSet or offsetUnset) throws a PayloadException internally, which is translated to the neutral output line 'error=immutable\n'. Both set and unset operations are blocked.

trigger_keywords: what error is returned for an expired token
what happens if exp is in the past
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If the exp claim is in the past (i.e., Utils::timestamp($payload['exp'])->isPast() is true), a TokenExpiredException is thrown, which maps to 'error=token_expired\n'. If nbf or iat is in the future, a TokenInvalidException is thrown, mapping to 'error=token_invalid\n'. Expiration is checked before the nbf/iat future-check logic (expiration is checked last among the timestamp checks).

trigger_keywords: what does payload_get return when select is absent
how is the whole claim set returned
what format is the full claim mapping returned in
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the select field is absent (null), payload_get returns the full claim set as a JSON object mapping claim names to their values in claim order (the order claims were built). When select is a single string, it returns the value of that one claim. When select is an array of strings, it returns a JSON array of the corresponding values in the requested order.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the token identity field must be derived deterministically from the subject and the not-before values
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The jti() method in PayloadFactory (golden/src/PayloadFactory.php) computes the jwt id as md5(sprintf('jti.%s.%s', $sub, $nbf)), where $sub comes from the claims array key 'sub' and $nbf from key 'nbf'.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check the source code in golden/src/ for authoritative implementation details, particularly Payload.php, PayloadFactory.php, and the Validators directory.

feature1_token_structure.json.description: A compact token is a single string of segments joined by dots. Validation accepts a string only when it has exactly three dot-separated segments and then yields the token unchanged; any other number of segments is rejected as a structurally invalid token.

feature2_1_get_values.json.description: A claim set is an ordered collection of named claims. Retrieval can return the whole set as a name-to-value mapping, the value of a single named claim, or — given a list of names — the list of their values in the requested order. Every value is emitted in its native JSON form.

feature2_2_membership.json.description: Given a claim set, report for each queried name whether a claim with that name is present, and for each queried name/value pair whether an equal claim belongs to the set. A pair matches only when both its name and its value match a member of the set.

feature2_3_get_by_type.json.description: Each claim carries a well-known type (such as subject, issuer or token id). A value can be fetched by naming the claim's type, returning the matching claim's value in its native JSON form. Naming a type that is not part of the claim set is an error.

feature2_4_immutability.json.description: A claim set is immutable. Any attempt to assign a value to a key or to remove a key is rejected with an immutability error and leaves the set unchanged.

feature3_1_structure.json.description: Structural validation requires that all mandatory claims are present: issuer, issued-at, expiration, not-before, subject and token id. A set that contains every mandatory claim passes structural validation; a set missing one or more mandatory claims is rejected as invalid.

feature3_2_timestamps.json.description: Given a reference current time (a Unix timestamp) and a claim set whose temporal claims are Unix timestamps, validation enforces the time window: an expiration in the past rejects the set as expired, while a not-before time or an issued-at time in the future rejects the set as invalid.

feature4_assemble.json.description: Assemble a complete claim set from a map of caller-supplied claims plus automatically filled standard claims, evaluated against a reference current time and an issuer URL. Any standard claim the caller omits is generated: the issuer is the given URL, issued-at and not-before are the reference time, the expiration is the reference time plus the configured lifetime (in minutes, default sixty), and a token id is derived deterministically. The result is the assembled claim set as a name-to-value mapping in which caller-supplied claims keep their given values and ordering.
